tip: The Russian word for 'hi' is one of the first words every learner masters. Use it freely with friends.

words.notes: Casual greeting among close friends. Drop it with elders.
words.image_prompt: two cheerful friends waving hello to each other in a sunny park

tip: Russians take formality seriously. Using the wrong greeting can feel rude.

words.notes: Standard polite greeting for any time of day.
words.image_prompt: a person bowing politely at an office entrance

words.notes: Say to the person leaving; if you leave, say 안녕히 계세요.
words.image_prompt: two colleagues waving goodbye at a train station

tip: The two most powerful tiny words. Master these and you can survive any conversation.

words.notes: Polite 'yes'. 응 is the casual form.
words.image_prompt: a smiling person giving a thumbs up with a checkmark

words.notes: Polite 'no'. 아니 is casual.
words.image_prompt: a person gently shaking their head

tip: These two words will instantly make Russians warm up to you.

words.notes: Formal thanks. 고마워 is casual.
words.image_prompt: two hands passing a small gift wrapped in ribbon

words.notes: Attach after a noun to make a polite request.
words.image_prompt: a waiter offering a cup of tea on a tray

tip: Both ways to apologize, but used differently — формал vs. casual.

words.notes: Formal apology. 미안해 is casual.
words.image_prompt: a person bowing slightly in apology

words.sentence_en: Excuse me, may I ask for directions?
words.notes: Polite way to get attention.
words.image_prompt: a person politely tapping someone's shoulder to ask directions

tip: The standard follow-up after 'hi'. Practice this phrase until automatic.

words.sentence_en: Long time no see, how are you?
words.notes: Common check-in. Reply: 네, 잘 지내요.
words.image_prompt: two friends meeting on a bridge asking how they are

words.notes: Doubles as 'I like it'.
words.image_prompt: a smiling sun over a peaceful flower garden

tip: Russian has TWO 'you' — one for friends, one for elders. Mix them up and you'll seem cold or oddly informal.

words.notes: Humble 'I' for polite speech. 나 is casual.
words.image_prompt: a person politely introducing themselves with a small bow

words.notes: Casual 'I' among friends.
words.image_prompt: a child pointing to themselves with a proud smile

words.notes: Formal but can sound distant; Koreans often use names/titles instead.
words.image_prompt: two people addressing each other formally

tip: Russian doesn't use 'to be' in present tense. 'He student' = 'He is a student'.

words.notes: 그 + 사람 = that person. Often 그 사람.
words.image_prompt: a young man waving from across a street

words.notes: Mostly written language; speech uses names.
words.image_prompt: a kind woman holding an open book

words.notes: Also used as 'my' for family/country (우리 나라).
words.image_prompt: four friends linking arms and laughing

words.notes: 들 marks plural. Mostly written.
words.image_prompt: a small group of students walking with backpacks

tip: Possessive words change shape based on what they modify. Don't stress, just notice the pattern.

words.notes: Humble 저 + 의 → 제. Polite form of 내.
words.image_prompt: a hand holding a small name tag

words.notes: 의 marks possession. Casual: 네/너의.
words.image_prompt: two hands exchanging a bag

tip: Russians love their parents. These words come up in every family conversation.

words.notes: Endearing. 어머니 is formal.
words.image_prompt: a loving mother hugging her child in a sunny kitchen

words.notes: Endearing. 아버지 is formal.
words.image_prompt: a father reading a bedtime story to his child

tip: Russian families are often close. Cousins and aunts are part of everyday life.

words.notes: Sino-Korean word (家族).
words.image_prompt: a three-generation family around a dinner table

words.notes: Used by males. Females say 오빠.
words.image_prompt: two brothers playing together

words.notes: Used by males. Females say 언니.
words.image_prompt: two siblings sharing an umbrella in gentle rain

tip: Russian grandparents (бабушка/дедушка) are warm, central figures — like in fairy tales.

words.notes: Warm cultural figure in Korean families.
words.image_prompt: a kind grandmother making rice cakes in a cozy kitchen

words.notes: Respected elder.
words.image_prompt: a wise grandfather with a gentle smile reading

tip: Russian distinguishes male and female friends clearly. The gender matters.

words.notes: Sino-Korean (親舊). Gender-neutral.
words.image_prompt: two friends sharing a meal at a casual cafe

words.notes: Native Korean word for person.
words.image_prompt: a friendly person smiling warmly

tip: Russian homes are warm refuges from cold winters. These words come up constantly.

words.notes: Also 'home' as a concept.
words.image_prompt: a cozy traditional house with warm light

words.notes: Sino-Korean (房).
words.image_prompt: a sunny tidy bedroom with a small bookshelf

tip: The kitchen (кухня) is the heart of any Russian home — where tea and stories happen.

words.notes: Native Korean word.
words.image_prompt: a warm kitchen with a steaming pot and rice

words.notes: Sino-Korean (化粧室). Essential travel word!
words.image_prompt: a clean simple bathroom door with a sign

tip: Pay attention to gender endings: -ь often means feminine, even on inanimate things.

words.notes: Sino-Korean (門).
words.image_prompt: an old wooden door slightly ajar

words.notes: 窓門. 창 alone also means window.
words.image_prompt: an open window with curtains fluttering, garden outside

tip: These words appear in nearly every sentence about daily life.

words.sentence_en: There's a book on the desk.
words.notes: 冊床. 상 = table.
words.image_prompt: a wooden desk with a vase of flowers and a book

words.notes: 椅子.
words.image_prompt: a comfortable wooden chair by a sunny window

tip: Russian literature is world-famous. Books are deeply respected in this culture.

words.notes: 冊. Koreans value reading.
words.image_prompt: an open antique book with golden pages

words.notes: Loanword from English.
words.image_prompt: a beautiful blue fountain pen on a letter

tip: Many tech words in Russian are borrowed from English — easy wins for learners!

words.notes: From 'hand phone'. Also 휴대폰.
words.image_prompt: a modern smartphone on a wooden table with coffee

words.notes: Loanword.
words.image_prompt: a laptop open on a cozy desk with a mug

tip: Bread (хлеб) is sacred in Russian culture. There's even a saying: 'хлеб — всему голова' (bread is the head of everything).

words.notes: Native Korean. Essential word.
words.image_prompt: a clear glass of water with droplets on a table

words.notes: From Portuguese 'pão' via Japanese.
words.image_prompt: a fresh loaf of bread sliced on a board

tip: Russians drink TEA, not coffee, traditionally. A samovar (large tea urn) is iconic.

words.notes: 茶. Green tea (녹차) is popular.
words.image_prompt: a warm cup of green tea with steam

words.notes: Common free drink at Korean restaurants.
words.image_prompt: a glass of golden barley tea on a table

tip: Coffee (кофе) is masculine but indeclinable — it never changes form. A small grammar gift.

words.notes: Loanword. Korea has huge cafe culture.
words.image_prompt: a steaming cup of coffee on a cafe table

words.notes: 牛乳, Sino-Korean.
words.image_prompt: a glass bottle of fresh milk in morning light

tip: Apples and bananas — easy starter fruits with simple pronunciations.

words.notes: 沙果. Also means 'apology' (homophone!).
words.image_prompt: a glossy red apple with a green leaf

words.notes: Loanword.
words.image_prompt: a bunch of ripe yellow bananas

tip: Russian cuisine traditionally features lots of meat, especially in winter.

words.notes: Native Korean. 불고기 = fire-meat.
words.image_prompt: a plate of grilled meat with vegetables

words.notes: 生鮮. Live fish is 물고기.
words.image_prompt: a fresh fish on ice with lemon and herbs

tip: Borscht is the most famous Russian soup — beet-based and ruby-red.

words.notes: Native Korean. Served with most meals.
words.image_prompt: a steaming bowl of Korean soup

words.notes: Korea's iconic fermented dish.
words.image_prompt: a bowl of vibrant red kimchi

tip: Russians have a serious sweet tooth — desserts are part of every gathering.

words.notes: Loanword.
words.image_prompt: a slice of chocolate layer cake with berries

words.notes: 沙糖 (sugar). Also 과자 = snacks.
words.image_prompt: a colorful pile of wrapped candies

tip: Russian's biggest gift to learners: NO 'is/am/are' in present tense. 'Я студент' = 'I student' = 'I am a student'.

words.notes: Attaches to nouns: 노운+이에요/예요.
words.image_prompt: a person looking thoughtfully in a mirror

tip: Russians don't say 'I have'. Instead, they say 'by me there is' (у меня есть). Strange but melodic.

words.notes: 살아요 (polite present).
words.image_prompt: a person opening curtains to a sunny morning

words.notes: Means both 'have' and 'there is'.
words.image_prompt: a person happily holding a small kitten

tip: Russian has SEPARATE verbs for walking vs. riding. Don't mix them up!

words.notes: 가요 (polite). 에 marks destination.
words.image_prompt: a person walking down a quiet street

words.notes: 와요 (polite present).
words.image_prompt: a person warmly welcomed at a doorway

tip: Russian conjugation has patterns — once you learn one verb, many others follow the same rules.

words.notes: 읽어요. 을/를 marks object.
words.image_prompt: a person curled up reading in a cozy chair

words.notes: Also means 'to use' and 'bitter'!
words.image_prompt: a hand writing a letter with a pen

tip: These two will be your most-used verbs as a beginner. Practice them daily.

words.notes: 말 = words + 하다 = do.
words.image_prompt: two people having an animated conversation

words.notes: 理解+하다. Casual: 알아듣다.
words.image_prompt: a person having a lightbulb moment

tip: These modal verbs unlock the ability to make polite requests in Russian.

words.notes: Often 싶다 is used: 가고 싶어요 (want to go).
words.image_prompt: a child looking longingly at a cake in a window

words.notes: Verb + ㄹ/을 수 있다.
words.image_prompt: a person extending a helping hand up a hill

tip: Russian has TWO 'to know' verbs — one for facts, one for people. Today's is for facts.

words.notes: 알아요. Opposite: 모르다 (not know).
words.image_prompt: a person studying with a glowing lightbulb

words.notes: 생각 = thought + 하다.
words.image_prompt: a person looking dreamily out a window

tip: Adjectives in Russian agree with the noun's gender. Masculine ends in -ый, feminine in -ая, neuter in -ое.

words.notes: 좋아요. Opposite: 나쁘다.

words.notes: 나빠요 (polite).
words.image_prompt: dark clouds and rain over a small house

tip: Russia is the largest country in the world (большая страна) — big is a big deal!

words.notes: 커요 (polite present).

words.notes: 작아요.
words.image_prompt: a tiny kitten sleeping in a teacup

words.notes: 새 (new) + noun is common: 새 차.

words.notes: For objects. People: 나이가 많다.

tip: These are compliments Russians love to give. Use them liberally.

words.notes: 예뻐요. Also 아름답다 (more formal).

words.notes: 재미 (fun) + 있다. Opposite: 재미없다.

words.notes: Native Korean.

words.notes: Also means 'chestnut' (homophone).

words.notes: Means both evening AND dinner.
